title: Infrastructure and Stewardship
subtitle: Confirmed assets, continuity, and the case for institutional stabilization
summary: A presentation defining Sutton SignWriting as existing infrastructure with real assets, active stewardship, and identifiable continuity risks.
synopsis: The infrastructure conversation becomes stronger when it moves from general concern to concrete authority. Sutton SignWriting already includes domains, servers, software, datasets, and standards under active stewardship, and the central challenge is now stabilization, documentation, and institutionalization rather than initial creation.

slides.title: From Fragility to Stewardship
slides.summary: The central issue is no longer whether SignWriting infrastructure exists, but whether it will be stabilized and institutionally supported.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for infrastructure and stewardship overview.
bullets: Existing infrastructure, not a speculative project
Active stewardship with identifiable responsibilities
Need for stabilization, documentation, and institutionalization
slides.speech: The infrastructure conversation changes once the starting point is stated clearly. Sutton SignWriting is not an idea waiting to become real. The infrastructure already exists across domains, software, data, standards, and public systems. The question now is whether that infrastructure will continue under a fragile maintenance model or be stabilized through stronger stewardship and institutional support.
slides.notes: Change the audience's mental model from invention to stewardship. Keep the tone authoritative, specific, and calm.
slides.mermaid:
graph TD
  A[Existing Infrastructure] --> B[Active Stewardship]
  B --> C[Current Fragility]
  C --> D[Need for Institutional Stabilization]
  D --> E[Durable Continuity]

slides.title: Confirmed Asset Classes
slides.summary: The stewardship case rests on named asset classes that are already in operation and already require continuity work.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for internal authority and asset inventory.
bullets: Domains and public web presence
Servers, software, and platform systems
Datasets, corpora, standards, and continuity responsibilities
slides.speech: The stewardship case becomes stronger when the asset classes are named directly. This is not only a public story about something that appears important. It is a concrete infrastructure picture that includes domains, servers, code, data, standards, and continuity responsibilities already in operation. Once those asset classes are visible, the institutional reading becomes much clearer.
slides.notes: Keep this concrete and transitional. It should point directly into the inventory that follows.
slides.mermaid:
graph TD
  A[Domains and Public Sites] --> F[Confirmed Asset Classes]
  B[Servers and Platform Systems] --> F
  C[Software and Code] --> F
  D[Datasets and Corpora] --> F
  E[Standards and Continuity] --> F
  F --> G[Institutional Credibility]

slides.title: Domains and Web Infrastructure
slides.summary: The infrastructure includes a coordinated portfolio of domains and public web entry points that already serve SignWriting users, tools, and historical material.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for domain and web infrastructure.
bullets: SignWriting, SignPuddle, SignBank, and related domains
Public presence, application entry points, and archives
Renewal, DNS, hosting, uptime, and migration responsibilities
slides.speech: The domain layer alone shows that this is already a living web infrastructure. The ecosystem includes SignWriting, SignPuddle, SignBank, Sutton SignWriting, Valerie Sutton, and related historical and project domains. These are not decorative assets. They are entry points for tools, documentation, archives, and public continuity, and they require active stewardship.
slides.notes: Mention examples without reading the whole list aloud. The point is coordinated scope, not memorization.
slides.mermaid:
graph LR
  A[Domain Portfolio] --> B[Public Sites]
  A --> C[Application Entry Points]
  A --> D[Standards and Documentation]
  A --> E[Historical Archives]
  B --> F[Stewardship Responsibilities]
  C --> F
  D --> F
  E --> F

slides.title: Server and Platform Infrastructure
slides.summary: SignWriting depends on long-running servers and mixed hosting environments that need continuity, backup planning, and modernization.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for server and platform infrastructure.
bullets: Legacy and active production systems
Static and dynamic hosting environments
Immediate concerns: backup, disaster recovery, migration, deployment knowledge
slides.speech: Behind the public websites is a real platform layer made up of legacy servers, active production systems, and a mixed architecture across older and newer environments. These systems support dictionary services, archival content, downloads, and developer resources. Their current value is clear, but so is the need for backup, recovery planning, and migration documentation.
slides.notes: This is the operational-risk slide. Keep it practical and avoid drifting into low-level implementation details.
slides.mermaid:
graph LR
  A[Legacy Systems] --> D[Service Continuity]
  B[Active Production Systems] --> D
  C[Platform V3 Redevelopment] --> D
  D --> E[Backup and Recovery]
  D --> F[Migration Planning]
  D --> G[Deployment Documentation]

slides.title: Software and Code Infrastructure
slides.summary: The codebase is a mature, cross-language technical environment with active development, production use, and long-term maintenance value.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for software and code infrastructure.
bullets: Core libraries in JavaScript, Python, and PHP
Rendering, fonts, web components, APIs, and platform work
Open-source, production-used, and actively maintained
slides.speech: The software layer is not a prototype environment. It includes core libraries, rendering systems, fonts, web components, APIs, and platform work across multiple languages. Organized through the Sutton SignWriting GitHub ecosystem and related repositories, this body of code is both operational and extensible. It represents a mature technical foundation.
slides.notes: Make the technical seriousness visible. Say 'mature technical foundation' clearly.
slides.mermaid:
graph LR
  A[Code Infrastructure] --> B[Libraries]
  A --> C[Rendering and Fonts]
  A --> D[Clients and Components]
  A --> E[APIs and Platform]
  B --> F[Production Use]
  C --> F
  D --> F
  E --> F

slides.title: Data and Corpus Assets
slides.summary: The ecosystem includes rare large-scale written signed-language data accumulated across dictionaries, SPML files, lexical resources, and texts.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for data and corpus assets.
bullets: SignPuddle dictionaries and SPML files
Millions of sign entries across languages
Lexical, textual, educational, and religious materials
slides.speech: One of the most significant parts of the infrastructure is the accumulated SignWriting data itself. This includes SignPuddle dictionaries, SPML files, millions of sign entries, lexical resources, and literature collections. Preserved across multiple migrations, this is a rare large-scale corpus of written signed language and an important research asset as well as a stewardship responsibility.
slides.notes: Make the corpus value felt. Here, infrastructure becomes scholarship and preservation at the same time.
slides.mermaid:
graph LR
  A[SignPuddle Dictionaries] --> E[Corpus Assets]
  B[SPML Files] --> E
  C[Lexical and Textual Resources] --> E
  D[Educational and Religious Literature] --> E
  E --> F[Preservation]
  E --> G[Standardization]
  E --> H[Research Access]

slides.title: Standards and Encoding Infrastructure
slides.summary: ISWA 2010, FSW, SWU, fonts, and rendering systems form a stable technical framework for machine-readable SignWriting.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for standards and encoding infrastructure.
bullets: ISWA 2010 symbol layer
FSW and SWU formal encodings
Fonts and rendering systems for production use
slides.speech: The standards layer is one of the clearest signs that this work has already become durable infrastructure. ISWA 2010, Formal SignWriting in ASCII, SignWriting in Unicode, and the associated font and rendering systems make SignWriting machine-readable, searchable, sortable, and processable. This is a complete technical framework, not an informal notation experiment.
slides.notes: Connect this slide to the standards pages on the site if useful. It helps show depth beyond the current presentation.
slides.mermaid:
graph TD
  A[ISWA 2010] --> B[Formal Model]
  B --> C[FSW]
  B --> D[SWU]
  C --> E[Software and Search]
  D --> F[Unicode Workflows]
  A --> G[Fonts and Rendering]
  G --> E
  G --> F

slides.title: Stewardship Continuity
slides.summary: The infrastructure already has an identifiable stewardship lineage, but the continuity model remains too informal for assets of this scope.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for stewardship continuity and lineage.
bullets: Valerie Sutton: originator and foundational system design
Steve Slevinski: technical steward, encoding architect, and maintainer
Next-generation continuity path exists but needs formal support
slides.speech: There is already a recognizable continuity chain behind this infrastructure. Valerie Sutton originated the system and its foundational design. Steve Slevinski has carried the technical stewardship, encoding work, and operational maintenance. A next-generation continuity path exists, but it remains too informal for infrastructure with this many public systems, standards, and datasets.
slides.notes: This is the human continuity slide. Keep it institutional and stewardship-focused rather than personal or territorial.
slides.mermaid:
graph TD
  A[Valerie Sutton] --> B[Foundational System Design]
  B --> C[Steve Slevinski]
  C --> D[Technical Stewardship]
  D --> E[Next-Generation Continuity Path]
  E --> F[Need for Formal Institutional Support]

slides.title: Risk Profile
slides.summary: The main risks are concentrated knowledge, too few maintainers, incomplete documentation, and limited institutional redundancy.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for infrastructure risk profile.
bullets: Operational knowledge concentration
Small maintainer base
Documentation and preservation gaps
Lack of institutional redundancy
slides.speech: With a more internal view, the risk profile can be stated clearly. The main issues are concentrated operational knowledge, dependence on a very small number of maintainers, incomplete documentation, and limited institutional redundancy. These are common infrastructure risks when systems grow in value faster than their administrative support.
slides.notes: Frame the risk as structural, not as individual failure. The phrase 'grown in value faster than its institutional support' is worth emphasizing.
slides.mermaid:
graph LR
  A[Concentrated Knowledge] --> E[Fragility]
  B[Few Maintainers] --> E
  C[Incomplete Documentation] --> E
  D[Limited Redundancy] --> E
  E --> F[Preservation Risk]
  E --> G[Service Risk]

slides.title: What a Host Institution Would Actually Be Doing
slides.summary: A host institution would not be funding an idea from zero; it would be stabilizing and extending an existing globally used infrastructure system.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for host institution role.
bullets: Lower startup cost than greenfield infrastructure
Higher grant readiness and collaboration potential
Immediate value in preservation, continuity, and research use
slides.speech: This level of asset maturity changes the institutional question. A host institution would not be funding an idea or starting from zero. It would be stabilizing and extending an existing globally used infrastructure system. That lowers startup cost while increasing grant readiness, collaboration potential, and research applicability.
slides.notes: Make the mental-model shift feel decisive and reassuring.
slides.mermaid:
graph LR
  A[Existing Assets] --> B[Host Institution]
  B --> C[Stabilization]
  B --> D[Documentation]
  B --> E[Research Integration]
  B --> F[Grant Readiness]
  C --> G[Durable Infrastructure]

slides.title: Bottom Line
slides.summary: The real task is not creation but stabilization, documentation, and institutionalization of infrastructure that already exists.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for bottom line of stewardship case.
bullets: Domains
Servers
Software
Datasets
Standards
slides.speech: The bottom line is straightforward. SignWriting infrastructure already exists across domains, servers, software systems, datasets, and standards. The task now is not creation. It is stabilization, documentation, and institutionalization. That is the stewardship challenge, and it is also the institutional opportunity.
slides.notes: End with the exact triad: stabilization, documentation, and institutionalization.
slides.mermaid:
graph TD
  A[Domains] --> F[Existing Infrastructure]
  B[Servers] --> F
  C[Software] --> F
  D[Datasets] --> F
  E[Standards] --> F
  F --> G[Stabilization]
  F --> H[Documentation]
  F --> I[Institutionalization]
